topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an oppressive intensity, reflecting off the glossy pages of my research notes. Every time I glanced at the empty chair beside me, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. Professor Albright was leaving. The news had trickled down through hushed corridors, and now, the silence where his guidance used to reside felt deafening. I traced the outline of my thesis proposal with a trembling finger. What if the direction he’d been steering me toward suddenly vanished? A wave of nausea washed over me, making the ink on the page swim. I stood up abruptly, needing to pace, needing to burn off this sudden, frantic energy. My breath hitched in my chest as I stared out the window at the indifferent campus quad, the familiar landscape suddenly feeling alien and threatening.

[story 2]

I kept rereading the last email, the one confirming his departure, over and over. My hands were slick with sweat as I gripped the worn edges of my laptop. Each sentence seemed to amplify the void his absence would create in my academic trajectory. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles vibrating beneath my skin. I kept checking my phone, half-expecting a message, a reassurance, anything to steady the internal tremor that was escalating with every passing minute. I took a deep, shuddering inhale, trying to anchor myself, but the air felt too thin. I walked to the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, wishing desperately that the reality of this impending change would simply dissolve.

[story 3]

The coffee in my mug had gone cold, mirroring the sudden chill that settled deep in my core. I kept re-reading the chapter outline, scrutinizing every footnote as if the answer to my entire future lay hidden within the margins. A restless energy made me pace the narrow confines of my dorm room, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the linoleum floor. I kept stopping, forcing myself to sit down, only to immediately feel the urge to move again, to do something productive, anything to distract from the hollow feeling blooming in my chest. I mumbled to myself, a low, tight sound, "This can't be happening," the words barely escaping before I quickly silenced them, my gaze darting toward the door as if expecting an unwelcome visitor.
